questions.question: A user submits a payment form. The browser double-submits accidentally (user clicked twice). Which HTTP method would cause a duplicate charge, and which would not?
options: GET would cause a duplicate; POST would not, because POST is idempotent
POST would cause a duplicate charge; PUT would not, because PUT is idempotent
DELETE would cause a duplicate; GET would not, because GET is safe
Both POST and PUT cause duplicates because neither is safe
questions.explanation: POST is not idempotent — each POST request is intended to create a new resource or trigger a new action. Submitting a payment POST twice creates two charges. PUT IS idempotent — PUT /payments/abc with the same body twice leaves exactly one payment record. This is why browsers warn 'Resubmit the form?' before retrying a POST, but will silently retry a GET. For payment APIs, idempotency keys are used to make POST effectively idempotent.

questions.question: What is the correct HTTP status code when a client POSTs to create a new resource and the creation succeeds?
options: 200 OK — the operation succeeded
201 Created — a new resource was created
202 Accepted — the request was accepted for processing
204 No Content — the operation succeeded with no response
questions.explanation: 201 Created is the specific success code for resource creation. It signals more than 200 OK does — it tells the client that a new resource was created (not just that the operation succeeded). By convention, a 201 response also includes a Location header with the URL of the newly created resource, so the client can retrieve it. 200 is for general success, 202 for async processing, 204 for success-with-no-body.

questions.question: Your server receives 'DELETE /items'. You support DELETE on '/items/{id}' but not on the collection '/items'. What response should you return?
options: 404 Not Found — the path /items doesn't exist
400 Bad Request — DELETE without an ID is malformed
405 Method Not Allowed — with an Allow header listing supported methods (GET, POST)
403 Forbidden — the client is not allowed to delete collections
questions.explanation: 405 Method Not Allowed is correct when the URL exists and is valid, but the requested HTTP method is not supported on that URL. RFC 7231 requires a 405 response to include an Allow header listing the methods that ARE supported. Here: 'Allow: GET, POST' since /items supports GET (list all) and POST (create new), but not DELETE. Using 404 would be incorrect — the path does exist.

questions.question: A client sends 'PUT /items/5' with body '{"name": "Widget"}' three times. Assuming your server implements PUT correctly, how many items with id=5 exist in your store after all three requests?
options: 3 items — one per PUT request
1 item — PUT is idempotent; calling it multiple times produces the same result as once
0 items — PUT deletes before recreating, so the final call leaves nothing
It depends on timing — concurrent PUTs may cause race conditions
questions.explanation: PUT is idempotent: it means 'set this resource to exactly this state.' Whether called once or ten times with the same body, the result is the same: item 5 exists with name 'Widget'. The first call creates it (returns 201), subsequent calls replace it with the same data (return 200). Only 1 item exists. Compare with POST: calling POST three times creates three new items with ids 1, 2, and 3.

questions.question: A client sends a POST with 'Content-Type: application/json' but the body is '{invalid json}'. What is the most appropriate response?
options: 200 OK with an error message in the body
500 Internal Server Error — the server crashed trying to parse JSON
400 Bad Request — the client sent malformed data that the server cannot process
415 Unsupported Media Type — the server doesn't understand JSON
questions.explanation: 400 Bad Request is correct: the client sent data that the server cannot process due to client error. The JSON is syntactically invalid — that's the client's fault, not the server's. 500 is for server-side failures (unhandled exceptions in your own code, database down, etc.). 415 would mean the server doesn't understand JSON at all, which is wrong since Content-Type was correctly set. The fix is to wrap json.loads() in try/except and return 400 with a descriptive error.
